---
title: "09 · Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem"
subtitle: "Spread of a distribution — and why averages are normal"
lesson-id: "t5.variance-clt"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t5.continuous-distributions"]
unlocks: ["t5.statistical-inference"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "databases"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [08 · Continuous Distributions](../08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $E[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx$?
3. Recall the average from [T1 L14](../../tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)?
:::

## Touch

Roll one die. Now average two dice. Now average thirty. The widget
shows the shape of each — and something striking happens to the
shape as you average more.

```{ojs}
viewof cltSel = Inputs.select(["one die roll: uniform 1..6", "average of 2 dice", "average of 30 dice (≈ normal)"], {value: "average of 30 dice (≈ normal)", label: "Distribution:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(cltSel) {
    case "one die roll: uniform 1..6": info = "PMF flat: P=1/6 each. Mean = 3.5. Variance = 35/12 ≈ 2.92. SD ≈ 1.71. Decidedly NOT bell-shaped."; break;
    case "average of 2 dice": info = "Triangular distribution centred at 3.5. Variance ≈ 1.46 (= 2.92 / 2). Already bell-curve-ish."; break;
    case "average of 30 dice (≈ normal)": info = "Approximately normal, mean 3.5, variance 2.92/30 ≈ 0.097, SD ≈ 0.31. CLT in action: averaging 30 i.i.d. variables looks Gaussian."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. The variance of one die roll is $\approx 2.92$. Of the average of
   30 dice: $0.097$. Why so much smaller?
2. The original distribution doesn't look normal — but the average
   does. Why?
3. As $n$ grows, the average converges to the mean (Law of Large
   Numbers). What's CLT's *additional* claim?

## Discover

### Problem 1

The **variance** of $X$ is
$$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - \mu^2.$$

Compute $\text{Var}(X)$ for $X \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 1]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\mu = 1/2$. $E[X^2] = \int_0^1 x^2 dx = 1/3$. So $\text{Var} = 1/3 -
1/4 = 1/12$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\text{Var}(X) = 1/12$.** Standard deviation $\sigma = 1/\sqrt{12}
\approx 0.289$.
</details>

### Problem 2

For independent $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ with mean $\mu$ and variance
$\sigma^2$, the **sample mean** $\bar X_n = (X_1 + \cdots + X_n)/n$
satisfies:
$$E[\bar X_n] = \mu, \quad \text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n.$$

Why does the variance shrink by $1/n$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance of sum (independent): $\text{Var}(X_1 + \cdots + X_n) = n
\sigma^2$. Then $\bar X = \text{sum}/n$ scales variance by $1/n^2$:
$n \sigma^2 / n^2 = \sigma^2/n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Variance of sum of independent = sum of variances.** Then
$\text{Var}(c X) = c^2 \text{Var}(X)$, so dividing by $n$ scales
variance by $1/n^2$.

This is the **Law of Large Numbers** intuition: $\bar X_n$ tightens
around $\mu$ as $n$ grows. Standard error: $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.
</details>

### Problem 3

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**: for i.i.d. $X_i$ with finite
mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$,
$$\dfrac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1).$$

In words: the sample mean (suitably standardised) tends to a *standard
normal* distribution, regardless of the original distribution.

Why is this remarkable?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Universality.** The original $X_i$ could be anything — uniform,
exponential, weird custom distribution — and the *average* still
ends up normal.

This is why the normal distribution dominates statistics: most
real-world quantities are *averages* (or sums) of many small
random influences, and CLT makes them all normal.

The CLT's proof uses *characteristic functions* (Fourier transforms
of densities) — Tier 6 / Tier 7 material.
</details>

### Problem 4

A coin is flipped 100 times. Number of heads $X \sim \text{Bin}(100,
0.5)$. By CLT, $X$ is approximately normal with mean $np = 50$ and
variance $np(1-p) = 25$.

What's the approximate probability of getting between 45 and 55 heads?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sigma = 5$. So 45-55 is within $\pm 1\sigma$. By 68-95-99.7 rule:
$\approx 68\%$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\approx 68\%$.** The binomial $\text{Bin}(100, 0.5)$ is well
approximated by $N(50, 25)$ for inference purposes.

CLT lets us use the normal distribution as a *practical
approximation* for many discrete distributions when $n$ is large.
</details>

### Problem 5

An ML model trained on different random subsets of data gives
different test accuracies. Each accuracy is a sample mean over the
test set. What does CLT say about the variability of these accuracy
estimates?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**The accuracy estimates are approximately normal**, with standard
error $\sigma/\sqrt{n_{\text{test}}}$ where $n_{\text{test}}$ is test-
set size and $\sigma$ depends on per-example accuracy variability.

This means: with a 10K-example test set, accuracies will vary by
roughly $\sqrt{p(1-p)/10000} \approx 0.005$ (half a percentage
point) at $p = 0.5$. **Reporting "85.36%" implies precision the
data doesn't support.**

Most ML papers' accuracy claims should report confidence intervals
based on this calculation.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The bell curve was first sketched in **De Moivre's** 1733 essay
*Approximatio* — he showed that the binomial distribution, for large
$n$, hugs an exponential-of-a-square shape. **Laplace** then built it
into his *Théorie analytique des probabilités* (1812), and a century
later the Russian mathematician **Lyapunov** proved (1901) the first
truly *general* central limit theorem — sums of independent variables
satisfying mild conditions converge to the normal, no matter how
weirdly distributed the summands. The "central" in CLT means
*foundational*, not "middle."
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The normal distribution is not built into nature — it is built into
**averaging**. Add or average enough independent random tugs of any
shape, rescale by $\sqrt{n}$, and the bell curve appears as if from
nowhere. That is why it pops up everywhere: heights, measurement
errors, polling, sample means — they are all averages in disguise.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Variance** $\sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - \mu^2$.

**Standard deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}}$.

**Properties:**

- $\text{Var}(c X) = c^2 \text{Var}(X)$.
- $\text{Var}(X + c) = \text{Var}(X)$ (shift invariant).
- For *independent* $X, Y$: $\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$.
- $\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2 / n$ (for i.i.d. sample mean).

**Standard error** of $\bar X_n$: $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ — measures
typical deviation of sample mean from true mean.

**Law of Large Numbers (LLN):** $\bar X_n \to \mu$ as $n \to \infty$
(in probability).

**Central Limit Theorem (CLT):**
$$\dfrac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1).$$
Sample mean is *approximately normal* with mean $\mu$ and SD
$\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, regardless of the original distribution (assuming
finite variance).

**Empirical rule** ($N(\mu, \sigma^2)$):

- $\approx 68\%$ within $\mu \pm \sigma$.
- $\approx 95\%$ within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$.
- $\approx 99.7\%$ within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

:::

> *Variance = expected squared deviation; SD has units of $X$;*
> *Sample mean has variance $\sigma^2/n$ (LLN);*
> *CLT: sample mean is approximately normal — universally.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A factory produces resistors with target resistance 100 Ω, SD 5 Ω.
> A sample of 25 resistors is tested. What's the SD of the *sample
> mean*?

$\sigma_{\bar X} = \sigma / \sqrt{n} = 5 / \sqrt{25} = 1$ Ω.

So the sample mean of 25 resistors is approximately $N(100, 1^2)$.
By 68-95-99.7: about 68% chance the sample mean is in $[99, 101]$.

This is the foundation of *quality control*.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\text{Var}(\text{constant}) = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0$.
</details>

**W2.** SD of $X \sim N(0, 9)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3$.
</details>

**W3.** Sample mean of 100 i.i.d. with $\sigma = 10$. SD of sample
mean?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$10/\sqrt{100} = 1$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** $X \sim \text{Bin}(100, 0.3)$. Mean and SD?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mu = np = 30$, $\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$.
</details>

**P2.** Coin flipped 1000 times. Approximate $P(\text{heads} > 540)$
using CLT.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\mu = 500, \sigma \approx 15.81$. Compute $z = (540 - 500)/15.81
\approx 2.53$. Standard normal table: $P(Z > 2.53) \approx 0.006$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\approx 0.6\%$.** Significantly more than 540 heads in 1000 fair
flips would be statistically very unusual.
</details>

**P3.** $\text{Var}(2X + 3Y)$ for *independent* $X, Y$ with
$\text{Var}(X) = 4, \text{Var}(Y) = 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2^2 \cdot 4 + 3^2 \cdot 9 = 16 + 81 = 97$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why does CLT need finite variance?** The Cauchy distribution
has *infinite variance*; the CLT fails for it. Sample means don't
narrow as $n$ grows.

What practical implication does this have for outlier-prone data?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Heavy-tailed distributions (Cauchy, Pareto, Lévy) violate CLT.**
For such data, sample means are unreliable: outliers dominate, and
larger samples don't help.

Practical examples: financial returns sometimes look like power-law
tails; risk models that assume normality (and thus CLT) underestimate
extreme losses (the *fat tail* problem). The 2008 financial crisis
exposed widespread misuse of the normal-distribution assumption.

For heavy-tailed data, use **median** (robust to outliers) instead
of mean, or *trimmed* / *winsorised* estimators.
</details>

**S2.** **Bootstrap confidence intervals.** Given a single dataset of
size $n$, you can estimate the SD of the sample mean (= standard error)
by *resampling*: draw $n$ items *with replacement* many times, compute
the sample mean each time, look at the SD of those many sample means.

Why does this work?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Empirical distribution → bootstrap distribution → sampling distribution.**
The empirical distribution (your dataset's frequencies) approximates
the true distribution. Resampling from it generates "synthetic
datasets" of the same size; their sample means approximate the
sampling distribution of the true sample mean.

By CLT, this sampling distribution is approximately normal with SD
$= \sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Bootstrap *empirically* estimates this SD
without needing to know $\sigma$.

Bootstrap (Efron, 1979) is the dominant practical method for
confidence intervals when no closed form exists. Used in nearly
every modern data-analysis pipeline.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Variance and SD are the same thing."** No — SD = $\sqrt{\text{Var}}$.
   SD has same units as $X$; variance has *squared* units.
2. **"CLT applies to any sum."** Needs *finite variance* of the
   summands and (technically) some independence / identical
   distribution. Heavy-tailed (Cauchy) breaks it.
3. **"Sample size $n = 30$ is enough for CLT."** Old rule of thumb;
   often true but can fail for very skewed distributions. Modern
   advice: check the actual distribution.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **[Variance](/glossary.qmd#variance) measures spread**, and
   [standard deviation](/glossary.qmd#standard-deviation) puts that
   spread back in the original units of $X$.
2. **Averaging shrinks variance by $1/n$.** The
   [standard error](/glossary.qmd#standard-error)
   $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ tells you how tightly the sample
   [mean](/glossary.qmd#mean) hugs the true mean.
3. **The [Central Limit Theorem](/glossary.qmd#central-limit-theorem)
   is universal:** averages of enough independent variables look
   normal, regardless of where they came from — provided variance is
   finite.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive $\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2/n$ from
   independence?
2. Without scrolling back, what's CLT's claim?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: sample mean is approximately normal regardless of original
distribution.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[10 · Statistical Inference](../10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (bootstrap).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[08 · Continuous Distributions](../08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[10 · Statistical Inference](../10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)** —
  applies CLT to confidence intervals.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Standard errors on accuracy / loss
estimates (P3, S2). Stochastic gradient descent inherits CLT-style
behaviour for batch averages. Many ML theorems assume CLT-like
concentration.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Statistics.** Hypothesis testing, confidence intervals,
$p$-values — all rest on CLT.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Finance & risk.** Heavy-tailed risks (S1) violate normality
assumptions; sophisticated models account for this.
:::
